title: Joojeh kabab
subtitle: Persian saffron and lemon chicken kebabs
excerpt: Chicken thighs marinated overnight in a mixture of saffron, lemon, onion, and olive oil, threaded onto skewers and cooked on a very hot griddle or grill until the outside is deeply charred and the inside stays moist. The saffron turns the chicken golden and the lemon keeps it sharp. One of the most popular Iranian kebabs.
sourceNotes: Joojeh kabab (Persian for 'small chicken kebab') is one of the classic Persian kababs, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Sabrina Ghayour's 'Persiana' (2014). It differs from shish taouk in using saffron as the primary flavouring and in the lemon-dominant marinade. The dish is a staple of Iranian restaurants worldwide.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate overnight for best results. The longer the marinade, the more the saffron penetrates.

content.text: The saffron must be properly bloomed. Grind the strands between your fingers before adding to 2 tablespoons of hot water and leaving for 10 minutes — the liquid should turn a deep orange-gold. This bloomed saffron goes into the marinade and also gets drizzled over the chicken while it cooks, deepening the colour and adding further fragrance. A pale marinade means insufficient saffron or insufficiently bloomed.

Use boneless thighs rather than breast. They remain moist under the intense heat of the griddle and have enough fat to caramelise properly. The lemon juice in the marinade partially brines the chicken, so the overnight marination produces a noticeably different texture from a short marinade. The griddle must be smoking hot before the chicken goes on.

items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, cut into 5 cm pieces
ground, bloomed in 2 tbsp hot water
bloomed in 1 tbsp hot water, for basting

content.text: Combine the bloomed {{saffron}} water, {{lemon}} juice, grated {{onion}}, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, salt, and {{black-pepper}} in a bowl. Add the {{chicken-thigh}} pieces and mix well to coat. Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.

Thread the marinated chicken onto skewers. Heat a griddle pan to the highest heat until smoking. Cook the skewers for 6 to 7 minutes per side, without moving, until deeply charred and cooked through. If basting, brush the chicken with the saffron butter mixture midway through cooking. Rest for 5 minutes before serving with warm flatbread, rice, and grilled tomatoes.

items.symptom: Chicken is pale rather than golden-saffron coloured
items.cause: Insufficient saffron or not properly bloomed
items.fix: Grind the saffron strands before blooming — this releases more pigment. The blooming water should be a deep orange before adding to the marinade. Use a generous pinch

items.cause: Pan not hot enough or chicken moved too soon
items.fix: The griddle must be at maximum heat before the chicken goes on. Do not attempt to move the chicken until it releases cleanly — this takes 6 to 7 minutes

items.cause: Overcooked or marination time too short
items.fix: Check at 12 minutes total — the juices should run clear but the meat should still feel slightly springy. Overnight marination makes the meat more forgiving as the lemon-saffron marinade adds moisture

items.cause: Too much lemon or insufficient marination time
items.fix: The lemon softens during a long marinade, tenderising the meat and reducing the sharpness. If too sharp after cooking, serve with plain yoghurt as a buffer

content.text: Joojeh kabab is the most popular chicken dish in Iranian restaurant culture, served alongside rice or bread with grilled tomatoes and raw onion. It occupies the same place in the Iranian grilled food tradition that shish taouk does in the Levantine one: the standard, the baseline, the thing that everyone orders. The saffron and lemon combination is distinctly Persian, as opposed to the yoghurt and tomato paste of the Lebanese shish taouk. In Iran, joojeh kabab is often cooked over charcoal, and the smoke from the coals adds another dimension that a griddle pan cannot fully replicate — but at high heat the result is close.
